summary: Gruber examines the fallout from Apple's decision to stop including FireWire cables and other accessories with new iPods, arguing that the controversy is more symbolic than substantive. He shows that the price cuts roughly offset the now-à-la-carte accessories, making it a packaging change rather than a technical one. He's more critical of the iPod Shuffle's FAT32-only disk format, which imposes Windows-era file naming restrictions on Mac users. He argues this is a genuinely un-Mac-like compromise that undermines Apple's 'it just works' philosophy, even if most users won't notice because the Shuffle is primarily a music player.
key_points: The FireWire cable removal is a marketing/packaging change, not a technical one — iPods still support FireWire, and price cuts offset the accessory costs
The real constituency being taxed is Mac users with older machines lacking USB 2.0, while the change saves Apple tens of millions on cables most Windows iPod users never used
The à la carte pricing is reasonable for the low-cost iPod Mini but feels stingy for the premium $449 iPod Photo
The iPod Shuffle's FAT32-only format is the more insidious issue, forcing Mac users to deal with archaic file naming restrictions that contradict Apple's ethos
Apple's own suggested workaround of creating zip archives to avoid file name limitations is an un-Mac-like kludge that typical users will never discover
money_quotes: Those cables never were free — you paid for them as part of the total price of the iPod kit.

A couple of bucks here, a couple of bucks there, and with over a million iPods being sold per month, you're talking about tens of millions of dollars on FireWire cables that most iPod users weren't using.
The a la carte accessory pricing seems reasonable with the low-cost iPod Mini; it seems stingy with the high-cost iPod Photo.
With apologies to Sigmund Freud, sometimes a cable is just a cable.
Remember the days when DOS/Windows file name restrictions were a target of mockery? With the iPod Shuffle, Windows' limitations are now ours, too.
But since when is Apple a company that takes the easy way out?

The 'it just works' factor has been one of the hallmarks of the Mac user experience since day one; using an iPod Shuffle as a disk drive forces you to be aware of arcane and bizarre file naming restrictions, and thus is utterly un-Mac-like.
I'm just saying it's a little sad when Apple Computer, of all companies, tells us that punctuation such as '=' and ';' are 'special characters'.
key_insight: The FireWire cable controversy is overblown symbolism, but the iPod Shuffle's FAT32-only format represents a genuine, if minor, betrayal of Apple's 'it just works' philosophy by importing Windows-era limitations onto the Mac.
